title: Severe Water Crisis Hits Dakshinpuri as Residents Struggle for Basic Dignity Amid Heatwave - illustrated Daily News
full_text:
Severe Water Crisis Hits Dakshinpuri as Residents Struggle for Basic Dignity Amid Heatwave
Thousands of residents in South Delhi’s Dakshinpuri area have been without regular water supply for over a month, forcing many to rely on public taps, purchased water, and neighbours for survival.
A worsening water crisis in Dakshinpuri has left thousands of residents struggling for access to basic necessities, including drinking water and bathing facilities, amid an intense heatwave gripping the national capital.
For nearly a month, residents across several blocks of the locality have reportedly been living without regular water supply in their homes. The shortage has severely impacted daily life for nearly 5,000 people, many of whom are now dependent on a single public tap outside a nearby temple.
Residents said women working as domestic helpers in affluent parts of South Delhi are being forced to request permission from employers to bathe at the homes where they work.
At the Shree Shiv Krishan Mandir near Block 10, residents gather every evening with buckets, drums, and plastic containers as soon as limited water supply begins flowing through the tap.
The rush often turns chaotic, with people scrambling to collect enough water for cooking, drinking, and toilet use.
Many residents complained that the water available is often black and contaminated, but they continue to store it because they have no alternative.
Sixty-five-year-old Urmila, who works as a domestic help, said she continues to carry heavy buckets of water up multiple floors despite severe body pain.
Another resident, Ishita, said the crisis has affected both her personal dignity and professional life. She revealed that she had persuaded employers in Kalkaji to allow her to bathe at their home due to the lack of water in her locality.
Several residents also said they have been purchasing water for weeks, adding financial strain to already difficult living conditions.
According to locals, repeated complaints to authorities have yielded little clarity regarding the cause of the disruption or when normal supply will resume.
A senior official from the Delhi Jal Board acknowledged the difficulties and stated that reduced availability of raw water from upstream sources has affected supply in parts of Delhi.
The official said approximately 6,500 water tanker trips are being carried out daily across the city to provide relief to affected areas.
However, residents argue that tanker supplies remain insufficient and irregular, especially for densely populated low-income neighbourhoods like Dakshinpuri.
The ongoing crisis has once again highlighted concerns over water infrastructure, urban inequality, and the impact of extreme heat conditions on vulnerable communities in the national capital.
